edges.description: After host-cell entry, Salmonella can persist and replicate intracellularly.
Intracellular persistence is reinforced by virulence factors that evade host immune responses.
Intracellular survival and replication support invasive disease and blood culture-positive enteric fever.
Immune evasion can facilitate invasive Salmonella infection and systemic bacteremia.
Blood culture-positive isolates can be assessed for clinically relevant antimicrobial-resistance mechanisms.
edges.target: Multidrug and extensively drug-resistant Paratyphi B

edges.source: Antibiotic therapy guided by susceptibility testing
edges.target: Multidrug and extensively drug-resistant Paratyphi B

edges.source: Antibiotic therapy guided by susceptibility testing

nodes.description: Antibiotic therapy is required for paratyphoid fever, but empiric choices should account for local resistance patterns and susceptibility results.
nodes.id: Antibiotic therapy guided by susceptibility testing

evidence.snippet: Empiric ciprofloxacin should be avoided due to persistently high resistance rates.
No resistance was observed for ceftriaxone or carbapenems.

nodes.description: Diarrhea is a frequent gastrointestinal manifestation.

evidence.snippet: Predominant clinical features included fever 100% (n = 424), vomiting/nausea 66.98% (n = 284), abdominal pain 68.86% (n = 291), and diarrhea 67.92% (n = 288).

nodes.description: Fluid replacement may be needed as supportive care when diarrhea, vomiting, or poor intake cause dehydration risk.

evidence.snippet: Predominant clinical features included fever 100% (n = 424), vomiting/nausea 66.98% (n = 284), abdominal pain 68.86% (n = 291), and diarrhea 67.92% (n = 288).

nodes.description: Mobile resistance elements can drive treatment-modifying antimicrobial resistance in S. Paratyphi B, including ramAp-mediated AcrAB-TolC efflux activation in XDR strains.
nodes.id: Multidrug and extensively drug-resistant Paratyphi B

evidence.snippet: Notably, these four plasmids carried the ramAp gene responsible for multiple antibiotic resistance by regulating the AcrAB-TolC pump, predominantly originating from China.

nodes.description: Salmonella enterica serovar Paratyphi (a Gram-negative bacillus) builds and maintains its peptidoglycan cell wall by penicillin-binding protein (PBP) transpeptidase cross-linking. This essential, bacteria-specific reaction is the target of the third-generation cephalosporin ceftriaxone, which retains activity against multidrug- and fluoroquinolone-resistant strains and is therefore a mainstay of susceptibility-guided therapy; acylation of the PBP active site halts cross-linking and is bactericidal.

evidence.snippet: although glycan chain polymerization occurs in the absence of transpeptidation (for example, in the presence of penicillin), efficient transpeptidation requires ongoing GTase reactions

nodes.description: Salmonella pathogenicity island virulence factors support adhesion and invasion of the intestinal mucosa after fecal-oral exposure.

evidence.snippet: Salmonella pathogenicity islands (SPIs), which are scattered throughout different Salmonella genomes and encode factors essential for adhesion, invasion, survival, and replication within the host.

nodes.description: Salmonella virulence determinants include genes that contribute to immune system evasion during pathogenesis.

biological_processes: symbiont-mediated evasion of host immune response

evidence.snippet: Plasmids can also carry various genes that contribute to Salmonella pathogenicity. For example, strains from several serovars associated with significant human disease, including Choleraesuis, Dublin, Enteritidis, Newport, and Typhimurium, can carry virulence plasmids with genes contributing to attachment, immune system evasion, and other roles.

nodes.description: After invasion, Salmonella virulence systems support intracellular survival and replication within host cells, including macrophage-associated niches.

evidence.snippet: Salmonella pathogenicity islands (SPIs), which are scattered throughout different Salmonella genomes and encode factors essential for adhesion, invasion, survival, and replication within the host.

nodes.description: After invasive infection, S. Paratyphi can be recovered from blood cultures, producing bacteremia as part of enteric fever.

evidence.snippet: The main objective of this study is to determine the prevalence and clinical presentation of Salmonella Typhi and Paratyphi bacteremia in cases of suspected enteric fever, and their antimicrobial-resistance pattern.
